sources_audited.note: BLS May 2023 NAICS 541200 table reports accountants and auditors at 31.73%, tax preparers at 7.13%, management at 10.54%, and office and administrative support at 28.76% of employment; it is a disclosed four-digit, employment-mix proxy rather than six-digit wage-weighted task evidence.

The paper's expansive model-zeta measure lists accountants and auditors at 100.0 and defines exposure as at least a 50% task-time reduction with LLM-powered software, while explicitly warning that this does not imply full task automation.

The report describes agentic bookkeeping, near-full tax-preparation automation subject to final review, methodical audit adoption due to regulatory and liability complexity, advisory modeling, and back-office workflow tools; it is directional industry evidence rather than a representative LMM outcome study.
The page states that enterprise-level GenAI use among tax, audit, and accounting firms increased from 8% in 2024 to 21% in 2025.
The article reports that one in seven respondents had a comprehensive AI strategy and identifies 462-plus responses across 25 countries, about half from US firms.

BLS projects 5% accountant-and-auditor employment growth from 2024 to 2034, cites economic and regulatory demand drivers, and says routine-task automation should raise efficiency and shift duties toward advisory and analytical work rather than reduce overall demand.

The NASBA paper states that most states require majority licensed-CPA equity and voting rights, at least 44 jurisdictions require active individual non-CPA owners, and alternative practice structures keep attest services under licensed-CPA control and oversight.

S&P describes a fragmented accounting, auditing, and taxation sector with stable customer bases and predictable recurring revenues and reports 24 global PE/VC-backed deals in the first 11 months of 2024; the deal evidence is broader than US LMM CPA firms.

The 2016 article reports roughly 850 survey respondents, 49% of multiowner-firm leaders in active merger discussions during the prior 24 months, one-fourth with current succession challenges, and another 48% expecting them within five years.
The compiled market article reports upwards of 115 CPA-firm deals in 2024; it does not provide a qualifying-control definition or an eligible-firm denominator.

The 2024 report states that 66% of respondents' firms offer hourly or time-based pricing, 62% flat fees, 48% value-based pricing, and 29% retainers; these are overlapping firm-level offerings, not revenue shares or measured AI-benefit retention.

AICPA reports that 81% of responses came from firms with revenue of $5 million or below, median net client fees grew 6.7%, and revenue growth occurred in audit and assurance, tax, and client accounting advisory services.
The IRS/National Taxpayer Advocate table shows professionally filed individual returns rising from 71.3 million in 2024 to 72.5 million in 2025, a 1.7% increase.

GAO states that more than half of individual taxpayers used a paid preparer in fiscal 2024 and discusses credentials, errors, and oversight; this covers all paid preparers rather than CPA firms or business-client services.

caveats: The a base is an honest but judgment-heavy translation from four-digit employment shares, a technical task-exposure rubric, and workflow examples; no source measures the wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated work in six-digit LMM CPA firms.

The rho range maps current adoption and strategy indicators to a five-year implemented share without longitudinal evidence on production penetration, realized hours saved, rework, or avoided headcount in the target firm band.

The e estimate is not directly measured: the cited sources establish ownership constraints, alternative practice structures, fragmentation, and recurring revenue, but not the share of $1-10 million EBITDA firms that pass target-specific eligibility screens.

The s5 proxy combines an old survey of merger discussions with broad recent deal compilations that lack a qualifying-control definition and eligible-firm denominator; the resulting five-year transfer probability remains weakly identified.
The q estimate is inferred from overlapping pricing-model offerings and nominal fee growth, neither of which measures discounted owner retention of AI benefits by engagement or revenue mix.

The d5 estimate relies on occupation employment, nominal firm revenue, and individual-return filing counts rather than a constant-price, constant-quality quantity measure for the full CPA-firm service basket.

The o estimate extrapolates from attest-control rules, accountant employment expectations, and all-paid-preparer usage to the year-five operator-required share across heterogeneous CPA services; none of the sources directly measures that share.
summary: Mechanics pass and all 14 primitive-cited sources are reachable and support the literal statistics or descriptions attributed to them. The default lens is coherent, evidence states and bridges are candid, ranges expose substantial uncertainty, and the narrative follows the registered evidence. The record remains estimate-heavy and several primitives are broad transformations rather than direct measurements, but those limitations are disclosed and do not constitute a material defect under the frozen test; publication with caveats is appropriate.
